formatted_address: 21106 Kenswick Meadows Ct, Humble, TX 77338, USA
pitch_rationale: The subject property's main hip roof (upper darker-shingle section within the orange dashed boundary) displays moderate shadow casting on its leeward planes relative to the building footprint, consistent with a mid-range pitch. The ridge is visibly set inward from the eave perimeter but not dramatically so, and the visible roof surface area from this near-nadir view is proportionally moderate — neither the broad flat appearance of a shallow 4:12 nor the compressed, shadow-dominated look of a steep 8–10:12. Neighboring homes in this Humble, TX suburban development show comparable dark-shingle hip roofs with similar ridge-to-eave geometry, reinforcing a 6:12 estimate. The lighter lower section of the dashed box appears to be an under-construction or low-slope accessory area and was excluded from the dominant pitch assessment. Geographic context (greater Houston area, warm climate, suburban tract construction) further supports 6:12 as the most probable pitch.
footprint_rationale: The subject home is inside an orange bounding box spanning approximately 255 px (W) × 268 px (H), equal to ~54.1 ft × 56.8 ft. The house is rotated ~35° off north. The roof has two identifiable sections: (1) a primary multi-plane hip-roofed section occupying the northern ~60% of the box (~210 × 175 px, 82% fill ≈ 1,357 sq ft) and (2) a lighter-toned attached addition or flat-roof section in the southern ~40% (~175 × 110 px, 88% fill ≈ 763 sq ft). Combined polygon estimate: ~2,120 sq ft; bounding-box × 72% fill gives ~2,213 sq ft — within 4%, so ~2,150 sq ft adopted. Edge estimates: two ridge sections totaling ~155 px (~33 ft); six hip lines averaging ~80 px each (~102 ft total); one to two valley lines where sections meet (~90 px, ~19 ft); minimal rake edges given hip construction (~25 px, ~5 ft); and perimeter eave lines (~780 px, ~165 ft). Confidence is moderate due to the diagonal roof orientation, possible active construction on the southern section, and shadow obscuring some lower eave lines.

findings.description: The entire roof surface within the bounding box is a bright white/light gray color — starkly different from every surrounding home's dark gray asphalt shingle roofs. This is consistent with a flat or very low-slope membrane roofing system (e.g., TPO or modified bitumen) rather than traditional pitched shingles.
Multiple small dark circular/dot-shaped objects are scattered across the light roof surface at irregular intervals — at least 6–8 visible items distributed across the field of the roof.
findings.location_description: Scattered across the central and south portions of the flat roof

findings.description: Large quantities of stacked lumber and construction materials are visibly piled along the east and south perimeter of the subject property, directly adjacent to the structure.
findings.location_description: East and south perimeter of subject property, at roof/ground level

condition.rationale: The subject roof within the orange bounding box is unmistakably different from all neighboring homes, which uniformly feature dark gray pitched asphalt shingle roofs. The subject roof presents a very bright white/light gray flat or very low-slope surface — characteristic of a membrane roofing system (TPO, EPDM, or modified bitumen). Multiple small dark objects are scattered across the roof at irregular intervals, which are visible at this resolution and warrant a closer look. Most notably, large piles of lumber and construction materials are stacked around the building's perimeter, strongly indicating this property is either under active construction or undergoing a major renovation/re-roof. Because the roofing system type and completion status cannot be fully determined from aerial imagery, and given the active construction context, this roof is rated 'concerning' and an in-person inspection is essential to confirm roofing material, installation stage, and any existing condition issues.
